albums.title: Discovery
albums.reason: Digital Love, One More Time, Superheroes — three of your most-played tracks ever all live here. It's been a while since you sat with the whole thing front-to-back.

albums.title: In Rainbows
albums.reason: Reckoner is your third most-played track of all time and it lives here. The full album deserves a proper revisit — it's one of their warmest and most textured records.

albums.title: The Dark Side of the Moon
albums.reason: Pink Floyd sits at nearly 1200 all-time plays for you, and this is the bedrock of that relationship. A record built for deep listening sessions.

albums.title: Melody A.M.
albums.reason: With over 1000 all-time plays, Röyksopp is a long-standing love — and this debut is the one that started it all, downtempo and gorgeous from start to finish.

albums.title: Moon Safari
albums.reason: Air has over 1000 plays in your all-time list and this record is their masterpiece — lush, unhurried, and perfect for the kind of mood you've been in lately.

albums.title: Leftism
albums.reason: Release the Pressure is one of your most-played tracks ever and it's the crown jewel of this album. Leftism is a full-album experience that rewards patience.

albums.title: Brown Album
albums.reason: Orbital sits at 777 all-time plays for you — and this record is their most emotionally rich, sitting right in that space between dancefloor and headphone listening.

albums.title: James Blake
albums.reason: Postpone alone has 156 plays — your most-played track ever — and CMYK is close behind at 90. This debut is where both live, and it's worth revisiting as a whole.

albums.title: II
albums.reason: You love Radiohead's tension and Daft Punk's electronic architecture — Moderat sit right in that space, electronic and brooding with real emotional weight.

albums.title: Music Has the Right to Children
albums.reason: If Röyksopp's Melody A.M. and Air's Moon Safari are comfort listens for you, this is the missing third piece — warm, slightly off-kilter, and utterly timeless.

albums.title: Mezzanine
albums.reason: Your all-time love for Radiohead and Leftfield points directly here — Mezzanine is where trip-hop gets gothic and confrontational, and it's one of the great headphone albums.

albums.title: Cosmogramma
albums.reason: You love James Blake's broken soul and Kanye's sonic ambition — Flying Lotus sits at that intersection, jazz-fractured and restless, unlike anything else in its era.

albums.title: Swim
albums.reason: Your love for Daft Punk's lush production and Pink Floyd's psychedelic drift points to Caribou — Swim is where those two worlds collide in the most beautiful way.

albums.title: Crooks & Lovers
albums.reason: James Blake's CMYK and the post-dubstep world around it leads straight here — Mount Kimbie's debut is raw, warm, and lives in a similar emotional space to early Blake.

albums.title: Supermodified
albums.reason: If you love Orbital's layered construction and Justice's raw electronic energy, Amon Tobin's most accessible record will hit hard — intricate, jazz-inflected, and restless.

albums.title: The Last Resort
albums.reason: Your love for Underworld and the deeper, moodier side of Leftfield points here — Trentemøller's debut is a moody, cinematic electronic record with real emotional range.

albums.title: Knee-Deep in the North Sea
albums.reason: You've been deep into Nils Frahm, Hania Rani, and Oscar Peterson this year — Portico Quartet live in that space between modern classical and jazz, meditative and quietly beautiful.

albums.title: Black Focus
albums.reason: Your recent pull toward Bill Withers, Charles Bradley, and the soul-meets-electronic world of DJ Koze points here — Yussef Kamaal fuse jazz, funk, and electronic into something urgent.

albums.title: v2.0
albums.reason: The Oscar Peterson Trio and Nils Frahm have both been heavy in your rotation — GoGo Penguin sit right between them, acoustic jazz with the rhythmic intensity of electronic music.

albums.title: The Universe Smiles Upon You
albums.reason: Your recent taste arc — Bill Withers, Sault, Nightmares on Wax — points to something warm, groove-based, and slightly hallucinatory. Khruangbin's debut delivers exactly that.

albums.title: Persona
albums.reason: You've been spending time with Apparat, Max Cooper, and Martin Kohlstedt — Rival Consoles lives in that same emotionally precise electronic space, melodic but with real grit.

albums.title: Portraits
albums.reason: Your Bonobo like (Kiara) and recent love for Weval, DJ Koze, and Nightmares on Wax all point here — Maribou State make warm, soul-flecked electronic music with serious emotional depth.

albums.title: Immunity
albums.reason: You've been living in Apparat, Max Cooper, and Dominik Eulberg — Jon Hopkins' Immunity is in that same lane but hits harder, moving from delicate ambience to full dancefloor catharsis.

albums.title: Elaenia
albums.reason: Four Tet, Brambles, and Weval have all been in heavy rotation for you — Floating Points' debut sits at that same intersection of jazz, ambient, and electronic, and it's a modern classic.

albums.title: Prehension
albums.reason: Joep Beving is your most-played artist this week with 15 plays — Liminal is in your recent pile, so this earlier record is the natural companion, just as stripped back and absorbing.

albums.title: Waltz for Debby
albums.reason: Bill Evans has been a clear presence this month — New Jazz Conceptions is already in your played pile, and this live trio record is widely considered his warmest and most intimate.

albums.title: We Get Requests
albums.reason: You've played Night Train 36 times in the last month alone — We Get Requests is the natural next step, a little more relaxed and melodic but equally elegant.

albums.title: Geography
albums.reason: Full Circle is already in your recent plays — Geography is where Tom Misch fully arrived, and it sits perfectly alongside the warm, jazz-touched mood you've been in lately.

albums.title: Solo
albums.reason: Nils Frahm has been a constant presence this month and week — with Screws and All Melody already played recently, Solo is the intimate, unadorned side of him you haven't revisited yet.

albums.title: Little Ghost
albums.reason: You played Voyager recently and Moonchild has been part of this month's mood — their debut is softer and more jazz-leaning, perfect for the contemplative headspace you're in.

albums.title: Smokers Delight
albums.reason: In A Space Outta Dub is already in your recent pile — this is the earlier, hazier classic, the one that defined their sound, and it fits the late-night groove of your current week.

albums.title: Fort Bellefleur
albums.reason: You played You Never Told Me I'll Miss You That Much this week from this very album — it's clearly landing right now, so the full record deserves a proper sit-through.

albums.title: South Wind, Clear Sky (Part 1)
albums.reason: scruscru has been all over your last 7 days — you've been picking individual tracks from this record, so pulling it up as a full listen feels like the right move right now.

songs.title: Harder Better Faster Stronger
songs.reason: You've played half of Discovery in individual tracks over the years — this one deserves a revisit as a standalone, it's still one of the most perfectly constructed electronic tracks ever made.

songs.title: Nude
songs.reason: Reckoner is your third most-played track ever, and Nude sits right beside it on In Rainbows — slower, more heartbreaking, and criminally under-revisited.

songs.title: Dark & Long
songs.reason: Born Slippy has 88 all-time plays for you — but Dark & Long is the deeper, slower Underworld cut that rewards patience, and it's one of their most transportive tracks.

songs.title: Remind Me
songs.reason: With over 1000 Röyksopp plays in your all-time history, this is one of their most enduring tracks — melancholy, beautiful, and built for exactly the kind of mood you've been in.

songs.title: The Rain Song
songs.reason: Led Zeppelin sits at nearly 1000 all-time plays for you — this is one of their most underrated and emotionally vast tracks, a long way from the blues-rock bombast.

songs.title: Phantom
songs.reason: Justice has 771 all-time plays in your library — Phantom is their most cinematic and underplayed track, a slow-building beast that sits apart from the obvious singles.

songs.title: Playground Love
songs.reason: Air has over 1000 all-time plays for you, and this Virgin Suicides soundtrack standout is one of their most quietly devastating tracks — worth pulling out on its own.

songs.title: Under Cover of Darkness
songs.reason: The Strokes sit at 652 all-time plays — this later single captures them at their most energetic and uncomplicated, a reminder of what made them so thrilling.

songs.title: Kong
songs.reason: You liked Kiara, which signals Bonobo is landing — Kong is a deeper cut from Black Sands, more rhythmically driven and just as textured, a natural next step from that like.

songs.title: Monument
songs.reason: Your deep Röyksopp love and the emotional scale of your all-time electronic taste points directly here — Monument is an eight-minute slow-build that rewards every second.

songs.title: Ascending
songs.reason: Your love for the deeper, stranger side of Orbital and Leftfield points toward Actress — Ascending is his most accessible and emotionally affecting track, haunting and beautiful.

songs.title: She Just Likes to Fight
songs.reason: Your all-time taste in textured, melodic electronic music — Daft Punk, Röyksopp, Orbital — leads here. This is one of Four Tet's most beloved and emotionally direct tracks.

songs.title: Loud Places
songs.reason: James Blake's fractured soul and The xx's atmospheric cool both point toward Jamie xx — Loud Places sits right at that intersection, one of the most emotionally precise electronic tracks of the decade.

songs.title: Glue
songs.reason: Your all-time love for Leftfield and Orbital points straight to Bicep — Glue is a track built on the same emotionally charged rave nostalgia, and it's become a modern classic in that lineage.

songs.title: Paper Trails
songs.reason: Your love for Pink Floyd's cosmic drift and the motorik pulse in Underworld and Leftfield leads to Darkside — Paper Trails is their most hypnotic and transportive track.

songs.title: Inspector Norse
songs.reason: You love the euphoric side of Justice and the warmth of Air — Inspector Norse is the crossroads of those two worlds, a nine-minute piece of pure cosmic joy that became an instant classic.

songs.title: Lovers' Carvings
songs.reason: Your recent love for Bonobo (Kiara like), Nightmares on Wax, and organic electronic sounds points here — Lovers' Carvings is one of those rare tracks that feels warm and timeless all at once.

songs.title: Source
songs.reason: Your recent deep listening to Oscar Peterson, Bill Withers' soul, and the groove-forward energy of Sault all point toward Nubya Garcia — this title track is expansive, warm, and deeply alive.

songs.title: To Build a Home
songs.reason: Your recent pull toward Brambles, Hania Rani, and Ólafur Arnalds — music that uses space and restraint to devastating effect — leads directly to this track, one of the most emotionally direct in its genre.

songs.title: Haul
songs.reason: You've been spending time with Martin Kohlstedt, Apparat, and Brambles — Christian Löffler sits right in that space, melancholic minimal electronic with a deeply organic feel.

songs.title: Altes Kamuffel
songs.reason: Your recent plays of Dominik Eulberg, DJ Koze, and Max Cooper point toward Stimming — this track is warm, melodic, and beautifully crafted in a way that sits naturally alongside those artists.

songs.title: Giants
songs.reason: Your recent deep listening to Bill Withers, Charles Bradley, and Sault's soul-forward sounds points to Andreya Triana — Giants is understated, warm, and quietly devastating.

songs.title: After Work
songs.reason: You've been living in Weval, Apparat, and the introspective side of Max Cooper — Ben Böhmer's After Work sits in that same emotional space, melodic and quietly profound.

songs.title: Nakamarra
songs.reason: Your recent listening arc through Bill Withers, Charles Bradley, Sault, and the soul-adjacent electronic world of DJ Koze points here — Hiatus Kaiyote are in their own lane, and Nakamarra is their most immediately breathtaking track.

songs.title: Sleeping Lotus
songs.reason: You've played 15 Joep Beving tracks in the last week — this early track from Solipsism is the one that introduced him to the world, and it holds up as one of his most quietly profound.

songs.title: Peace Piece
songs.reason: Bill Evans has been a strong presence this month and Peace Piece is his most meditative solo recording — a single sustained improvisation that's perfect for the quiet, introspective mode you're in.

songs.title: Hymn to Freedom
songs.reason: You've been all over the Oscar Peterson Trio this month — Hymn to Freedom is a less-played gem that carries a different emotional weight to Night Train, solemn and uplifting at once.

songs.title: Before Paris
songs.reason: Full Circle is in your recent plays and Tom Misch is part of this month's groove — Before Paris from Geography is one of his most effortlessly cool tracks, perfect for the current mood.

songs.title: Says
songs.reason: Nils Frahm has been consistent across your month and week — Says is his most cathartic and widely loved track, a slow build that pays off completely, and it feels right for right now.

songs.title: The Gift of Acceptance
songs.reason: You've been playing Fort Bellefleur this week and this track is one of its emotional peaks — a moment of pure house euphoria that fits the warm, soul-forward groove of your current listening.

songs.title: Colophon
songs.reason: Martyn Heyne has been in your recent plays and Electric Intervals is part of your recent album pile — Colophon is a standalone single that sits beautifully alongside the quiet, introspective sounds of this week.

songs.title: JSM
songs.reason: scruscru has been all over your last 7 days and you've been picking tracks from the JSM release — this title track is one of the most grounded and groove-forward of the set, worth singling out.

djSets.setName: HMWL Podcast 430: Niconé & David Hasert (Kompakt / Get Physical / HMWL)
Daphni - FOLD, London. Nov 22, 2025. 7hr DJ set
Klausmarie b2b Alec Troniq - RAVIN' RAVIN' 2026 @ Sektor Evolution
BENT THE KNEE TO HER VOL. 1
